The stream gage at the Smith River near Woolwine (SMWV2) rose to 11.04 feet, just above moderate flood stage due to this rainfall, however radar estimates put the highest amounts downstream of this gage.  Few reports of flooding impacts were received, however it was noted that Philpott Reservoir rose 3 feet in response to the inflows from the Smith River. In western Patrick County and southeast Carroll County, 4 to 7 inches of rain fell across the entire headwaters of the Ararat River, with radar estimates of around 9 inches near the town of Ararat, VA. Flash flooding in this area resulted in several roads being destroyed or heavily damaged where they passed over flooded creeks and rivers, including the Ararat River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Smith River near Woolwine (SMWV2) crested at 11.04 feet, slightly above the moderate flood stage of 11 feet. This is the highest crest since Sept 2018, and nearly a 10-year flood event per USGS StreamStats. The river rose nearly 9 feet in the 6 hours prior to the crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed within a warm, moist, and unstable atmosphere in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and/or hail up to half-dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew several trees down in the Chilhowie area. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the northwest central Virginia to the south of a stationary front draped across northern Virginia into central West Virginia. By early afternoon, a surface trough had developed along the lee side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, providing the lower atmospheric convergence needed to aid in thunderstorm development. Surface-based CAPE values were estimated to be in the 1,500 to 2,000 J/Kg range by noon just east of the Blue Ridge, while precipitable water values had risen into the 1.7 inch to 1.8 inch range, both of which supported heavy downpours and wet microbursts. A couple of the storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds which downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a tree which blocked both lanes of traffic at the intersection of Chestnut Street and Fourth Street in Fieldale. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, closed upper level low pressure system was observed over central Alabama on the morning of May 28th. The east winds around this low, allowed deep sub-tropical moisture from the Atlantic to move into lower Mid-Atlantic region. Areas of rain developed across the mountains by late morning, and gradually increased in coverage due to the sustained 25 to 30 knot (850mb) moisture inflow. Precipitable water values were observed in the 1.2 to 1.3 inch range through the day into the early morning hours of the 29th, which is around the 75th percentile for late May. Through the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th, the upper low drifted eastward, allowing instability to increase across the central Appalachians as the associated pocket of cooler upper level temperatures approached. While Surface-based CAPE levels remained minimal during the event, Most-Unstable CAPE (MUCAPE) values were observed climbing across the mountains through the evening of the 28th into morning of the 29th, reaching into the 600 to 850 J/Kg range. Combined with orographic lift, this instability supported the development of low-topped thunderstorms with enhanced rainfall rates.  As rainfall totals increased across the mountains and soils approached saturation, flash flood guidance values were lowered to under an inch at the 1- and 3-hour time periods. ||The heavy rain then led to flooding of Wolf Creek and Walker Creek. Elsewhere, training cells across Floyd and Franklin Counties resulted in minor flooding of the South Fork of the Roanoke River, which brought the Roanoke River up to Action Stage as it passed through the Cities of Roanoke and Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork Roanoke River near Shawsville, VA (SHAV2), crested at 5.64 feet (3,240 cfs) at 12:25 pm EST (1725 UTC) on the 29th above the flood stage of 5.0 feet. This event is between a 2-year and a 5-year event (50%-20% Annual Exceedence Probability) per USGS StreamStats.  Mainly only minor overbank flooding of properties along the river is observed at this stage, and no damage was observed or reported.

This is the 11th-highest documented crest of this gage, and the highest since February 2020. This event is nearly a 10-year event (10% Annual Exceedence Probability) per USGS StreamStats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A region of low pressure pushed a strong cold front eastward and lifted a warm front northward through the Mid Atlantic during May 16. More than sufficient instability and shear in the Virginia Southside resulted in a few rounds of discrete thunderstorms that organized into a quasi-linear structure, which resulted in areas of thunderstorm wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were snapped by thunderstorm winds near the intersection of Peppers Ferry Road and Virginia Route 613.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of surface high pressure centered over the eastern Great Lakes and Tropical Storm Idalia lifting northeast then east off the North Carolina outer banks, resulted in very strong northeast winds which caused minor to moderate (tidal) coastal flooding over portions of Virginia Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of surface high pressure centered over the eastern Great Lakes and Tropical Storm Idalia lifting northeast then east off the North Carolina outer banks, resulted in very strong northeast winds which caused minor to moderate (tidal) coastal flooding over portions of Virginia Beach. Chesapeake Bay Bridge Tunnel reached 5.51 feet MLLW at 930 am on Thursday, August 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of training thunderstorms produced extremely high rainfall across portions of Patrick and Carroll Counties during the predawn hours of August 28th. This resulted in areas of flash flooding, mainly in the headwaters of the Smith River and the Ararat River. ||The event began with a stationary front draped near the border of Virginia and North Carolina early on the 28th as an upper level disturbance began passing across the lower Great Lakes, placing the central Appalachians in an area of lift. The disturbance triggered a southeasterly wind shift across the foothills of the Blue Ridge in southern Virginia and northern North Carolina, bringing an increase of deep moisture northeast from the Piedmont of North Carolina. Coverage and intensity of the slow-moving activity only increased as the evening progressed, with a band of showers and embedded storms forming along the Highway 58 corridor by midnight, that was advancing northeast toward the crest of the Blue Ridge. Deep moisture combined with orographic lift to produce extremely efficient rainfall rates that were, at times, ranging from 3 to 5 inches per hour across Patrick County into neighboring Carroll County. Doppler Radar estimated rainfall totals of nearly 11 inches of rainfall in northern Patrick County across the Smith River basin, which was confirmed by one CoCoRaHS report of 10.70 inches, as well as a Cooperative Observer report from Woolwine of 10.62 inches. Flash flooding in this area resulted in several roads being destroyed or heavily damaged where they passed over flooded creeks and rivers, including the Ararat River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer at Woolwine, VA (WLWV2) measured a 24-hour rainfall total of 10.62 inches, a new all-time record for a 24-hour precipitation total for this station. However, the vast majority of this rainfall fell within a 6-hour window per MRMS data, which would make this total greater than a 1,000-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 point precipitation frequency estimates.  Runoff from this location flows into the Smith River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was established over New England, which extended southward across the lower Mid-Atlantic in a wedging pattern, with east windflow observed across Virginia and northern North Carolina. A cold front stretched from western Pennsylvania through eastern Tennessee, making slow eastward progress. The easterly windflow was tapping a pool of deep moisture from eastern North Carolina and Virginia, where precipitable water values ranged between 1.7 and 1.8 inches, while afternoon surface-based CAPE values ranged between 1,500 and 2,000 J/Kg across Smyth County. With weak winds aloft, storms across western Virginia were slow-moving. The combination of deep instability driven strong afternoon heating, high atmospheric moisture and slow storm motion resulted in a strong thunderstorm that produced 3 to 4 inches of rainfall over the town of Saltville  during a roughly 60-minute period, with rainfall rates of 4 inches per hour at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in localized flash flooding along Palmer Avenue in the community of Saltville.  Water was reported to have entered a few businesses along the road.  Property damage is the estimated cost of the damage and cleanup to the businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of surface high pressure centered over the eastern Great Lakes and Tropical Storm Idalia lifting northeast then east off the North Carolina outer banks, resulted in very strong northeast winds which caused minor to moderate (tidal) coastal flooding over portions of Northampton county adjacent to the Chesapeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of surface high pressure centered over the eastern Great Lakes and Tropical Storm Idalia lifting northeast then east off the North Carolina outer banks, resulted in very strong northeast winds which caused minor to moderate (tidal) coastal flooding over portions of Northampton county adjacent to the Chesapeake Bay. Kiptopeke reached 5.05 feet MLLW at 942 am on Thursday, August 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the northwest central Virginia to the south of a stationary front draped across northern Virginia into central West Virginia. By early afternoon, a surface trough had developed along the lee side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, providing the lower atmospheric convergence needed to aid in thunderstorm development. Surface-based CAPE values were estimated to be in the 1,500 to 2,000 J/Kg range by noon just east of the Blue Ridge, while precipitable water values had risen into the 1.7 inch to 1.8 inch range, both of which supported heavy downpours and wet microbursts. A couple of the storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds which downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree 2300 block of Walker Road. The lightning then traveled across the ground and impacted a breaker box of a neighboring residence. Emergency services were contacted and arrived on the scene to find a smoky haze inside. The local electric company also inspected the home, and electrician was needed to restore power to the residence. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A region of low pressure pushed a strong cold front eastward and lifted a warm front northward through the Mid Atlantic during May 16. More than sufficient instability and shear in the Virginia Southside resulted in a few rounds of discrete thunderstorms that organized into a quasi-linear structure, which resulted in areas of thunderstorm wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down about twenty trees along Ringgold Church Road, from the Barker Road intersection to the Countryside Drive Intersection. A large tree also fell down on a roof of a house and another tree fell onto a large propane tank.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a few trees on Bethlehem Church Road and Huckleberry Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, closed upper level low pressure system was observed over central Alabama on the morning of May 28th. The east winds around this low, allowed deep sub-tropical moisture from the Atlantic to move into lower Mid-Atlantic region. Areas of rain developed across the mountains by late morning, and gradually increased in coverage due to the sustained 25 to 30 knot (850mb) moisture inflow. Precipitable water values were observed in the 1.2 to 1.3 inch range through the day into the early morning hours of the 29th, which is around the 75th percentile for late May. Through the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th, the upper low drifted eastward, allowing instability to increase across the central Appalachians as the associated pocket of cooler upper level temperatures approached. While Surface-based CAPE levels remained minimal during the event, Most-Unstable CAPE (MUCAPE) values were observed climbing across the mountains through the evening of the 28th into morning of the 29th, reaching into the 600 to 850 J/Kg range. Combined with orographic lift, this instability supported the development of low-topped thunderstorms with enhanced rainfall rates.  As rainfall totals increased across the mountains and soils approached saturation, flash flood guidance values were lowered to under an inch at the 1- and 3-hour time periods. ||The heavy rain then led to flooding of Wolf Creek and Walker Creek. Elsewhere, training cells across Floyd and Franklin Counties resulted in minor flooding of the South Fork of the Roanoke River, which brought the Roanoke River up to Action Stage as it passed through the Cities of Roanoke and Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy runoff from rainfall flowing down a hillside caused the back wall of a building to collapse.  No injuries were reported.

No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 13 was partially blocked by downed tree limbs at Gorton Town Road. Numerous power outages were also reported in the area by local energy cooperative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed within a warm, moist, and unstable atmosphere in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and/or hail up to half-dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew multiple trees down along Penn Store Road and also a single tree along Old Well Road. The downed tree along Old Well Road fell on a home, trapping, but not injuring, one resident. Damages are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a large tree down across a power line along Marigold road, resulting in a power outage in the area. Additionally, thunderstorm winds blew a tree down near the intersection of Highway 58 and Soapstone Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south through Virginia, West Virginia, and Kentucky during the afternoon hours, approaching Tennessee and North Carolina. Storms formed a bowing line in advance of this front. Portions of this line produced damaging winds which downed some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple trees in Hungry Mother State Park. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms developed within an unstable airmass over Southside Virginia during the late afternoon hours. One of these storms increased to severe levels and produced wind gusts which downed trees just northwest of the City of Martinsville in Henry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down which blocked Smith Street. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several portions of Falls Mills Road were flooded and impassible due to the Bluestone River rising out of its banks onto the roadway.  No damage was reported to the road, and the road was reopened after the flooding receded.  The flooded road caused local residents to be stranded on their properties.  At least on home along the road was evacuated by a swift water rescue team as a precaution due to a resident being concerned that flooding would enter their home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of multiple low pressure systems near the Mid-Atlantic during the month of December brought abundant precipitation to central and western portions of Virginia, much of which fell as rain, through also with modest amounts of wintry precipitation. Precipitation totals varied generally from 2.5 to 3 inches near the state border with West Virginia to widespread five to six inches for most of the Piedmont.  Localized totals in excess of seven inches were recorded near the crest of the Blue Ridge in Patrick County. These precipitation totals were less than 75 percent of normal across the upper Clinch and Bluestone River basins in Tazewell County. However, most of the remainder of western and central Virginia observed amounts ranging from 100 percent to 150 percent, with locally higher amounts.  This made for vast improvements in local stream flows, ground water levels and soil moisture across the service area from their peak dryness in mid-November.  As such, where Severe Drought conditions were in place across most of the western and central portions of the state at the beginning of the month, conditions improved to a combination of Moderate Drought and Abnormally Dry conditions, leaving only the northeast corner of Bath County in Severe Drought status by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions already in place at the beginning of the month for Henry County persisted until December 12th, when two low pressure systems passing across the lower Mid-Atlantic during the period of December 3rd to the 10th brought rainfall amounts ranging from 2 to 2.5 inches to the county. This precipitation resulted in an increase in soil moisture, surface stream flows, and ground water levels sufficient to allow drought conditions for the entire county to be improved to Moderate Drought per the U.S. Drought Monitor. Additional precipitation of one to two inches during the following week resulted in removal of drought for the entire county, resulting in improvement to Abnormally Dry conditions for Henry County through the remainder of the month. Monthly precipitation totals ranged from 4.5 inches to locally 6.5 inches. The precipitation moistened leaf litter and other fuels that support wildland fire activity. There was no reported impacts to agriculture in December, with some farmers reporting that the lack of soggy ground make work in the fields easier to accomplish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low moved over the Great Lakes bringing four to ten inches of upslope snow across the region. Winds gusted to 40 to 50 mph during the event resulting in widespread blowing and drifting snow.

A large low pressure system propelling through the Upper Great Lakes resulted in a multi-day snow event down in the southern coalfields of southwest Virginia. Snow began to fall during the early afternoon on December 18th and continued into the morning of December 19th. The heaviest snow fell overnight with event snowfall totals ranging from 1 to 3 inches in Buchanan and Dickenson Counties. Remnant snow showers gradually dissipated on the afternoon of the 19th once high pressure began to slide into the area.
